title: Area Charts
text: Area charts line charts area line filled. ideal showing values change time emphasizing magnitude change. Stacking areas reveals composition whole.

title: Area Charts
code: library(muiCharts) library(dplyr)

title: Area Charts
text: Character count per Star Wars episode — shaded area emphasizes total across saga:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(     list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")   ),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Number of Characters", area = TRUE, showMark = FALSE)   ),   height = 300 )

title: Area Charts
text: Display multiple filled areas compare different film elements time:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(     list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")   ),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Number of Characters", area = TRUE, showMark = FALSE),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Number of Planets",    area = TRUE, showMark = FALSE)   ),   height = 300 )

title: Area Charts
text: Stack areas stack show totals part--whole relationships:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets),       Species    = lengths(species)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(     list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")   ),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Number of Characters", stack = "total", area = TRUE, showMark = FALSE),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Number of Planets",    stack = "total", area = TRUE, showMark = FALSE),     list(dataKey = "Species",    label = "Number of Species",    stack = "total", area = TRUE, showMark = FALSE)   ),   height = 300 )

title: Area Charts
text: Show individual data points alongside filled area:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(Starships = lengths(starships)) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(     list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")   ),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Starships", label = "Number of Starships", area = TRUE, showMark = TRUE)   ),   height = 300 )

title: Area Charts
text: Use curve = "natural" smooth area boundary:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(     list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")   ),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "# Characters", area = TRUE, showMark = FALSE, curve = "natural")   ),   height = 300 )

title: Area Charts
text: Map release year x-axis chronological view saga’s character growth:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       year       = as.integer(format(release_date, "%Y")),       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(year),   xAxis = list(list(     scaleType      = "linear",     dataKey        = "year",     label          = "Release Year",     valueFormatter = JS("(v) => String(v)")   )),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters", stack = "total", area = TRUE, showMark = FALSE),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets",    stack = "total", area = TRUE, showMark = FALSE)   ),   grid   = list(horizontal = TRUE),   height = 300 )

title: Area Charts
text: Missing values break area disconnected segments:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(partial = ifelse(episode_id <= 3, lengths(characters), NA)) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(list(     dataKey  = "partial",     label    = "Characters (Ep 1-3 only)",     area     = TRUE,     showMark = TRUE,     connectNulls = FALSE   )),   height = 300 )

title: Area Charts
text: AreaElement AnimatedArea sub-components rendered AreaPlot. Use slotProps configure composition chart:
code: ChartDataProvider(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   height = 300,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(type = "line", dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters", area = TRUE),     list(type = "line", dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets",    area = TRUE)   ),   ChartsTooltip(),   ChartsLegend(),   ChartsSurface(     AreaPlot(slotProps = list(area = list(skipAnimation = TRUE))),     LinePlot(),     ChartsXAxis(),     ChartsYAxis()   ) )

title: Area Charts
text: muiCharts integrates seamlessly muiMaterial create modern, fully-styled dashboards. muiMaterial’s Card, Typography, Stack components make easy add titles, subtitles, footers around charts. Combine ThemeProvider customize colors across entire dashboard: Stack component organizes title, chart, footer. Colors ThemeProvider reused chart. pattern works muiCharts component fits perfectly within Material Design ecosystem.
code: library(muiMaterial)  muiMaterialPage(   CssBaseline(),   {     primaryColor <- "#1976d2"     secondaryColor <- "#dc004e"      ThemeProvider(       theme = list(palette = list(primary = list(main = primaryColor), secondary = list(main = secondaryColor))),       Box(sx = list(p = 3, bgcolor = "background.paper"),         Card(sx = list(boxShadow = 3),           CardContent(             Stack(spacing = 1.5,               Typography("Film Elements Over Time", variant = "h6", sx = list(fontWeight = 600)),               Typography("Stacked area showing characters and planets", variant = "body2", sx = list(color = "text.secondary")),               LineChart(                 dataset = starwars_films |> mutate(Characters = lengths(characters), Planets = lengths(planets)) |> arrange(episode_id),                 xAxis = list(list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id")),                 series = list(                   list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters", area = TRUE, color = primaryColor, showMark = FALSE),                   list(dataKey = "Planets", label = "Planets", area = TRUE, color = secondaryColor, showMark = FALSE)                 ),                 height = 280               ),               Divider(),               Typography("Made with muiCharts and muiMaterial", variant = "caption", sx = list(color = "text.secondary"))             )           )         )       )     )   } )

title: Axis & Grid
text: MUI X Charts provide flexible axis configuration. article demonstrates axis customization based MUI Axis documentation.

title: Axis & Grid
code: library(muiCharts) library(dplyr)

title: Axis & Grid
text: scaleType property defines data maps axis positions: "band" — categorical (bar charts) "point" — categorical (line/scatter charts) "linear" — numeric (default value axes) "log" — logarithmic "sqrt" — square root "time" — date/time values

title: Axis & Grid
text: Discrete categories x-axis — ideal bar charts:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters")),   height = 300 )

title: Axis & Grid
text: Point scale places category discrete position — used line charts:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters", showMark = TRUE)),   height = 300 )

title: Axis & Grid
text: Numeric x-axis release year — spacing points reflects actual time gaps films:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       year       = as.integer(format(release_date, "%Y")),       Characters = lengths(characters)     ) |>     arrange(year),   xAxis = list(list(     scaleType      = "linear",     dataKey        = "year",     label          = "Release Year",     valueFormatter = JS("(v) => String(v)")   )),   series = list(list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters", showMark = TRUE)),   height = 300 )

title: Axis & Grid
text: Add labels axes using label property:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   yAxis  = list(list(label = "Number of Characters")),   series = list(list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters")),   height = 300 )

title: Axis & Grid
text: Customize axis values displayed using valueFormatter:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   yAxis  = list(list(     valueFormatter = JS("(v) => `${v} characters`"),     width = 100   )),   series = list(list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters")),   height = 300 )

title: Axis & Grid
text: Customize tick label appearance tickLabelStyle. Rotated labels work well long category names:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_starships |>     arrange(desc(crew)) |>     head(6),   xAxis  = list(list(     scaleType      = "band",     dataKey        = "name",     height         = 90,     tickLabelStyle = list(fontSize = "0.7em", angle = 90)   )),   yAxis = list(list(width = 60)),   series = list(list(dataKey = "crew", label = "Crew")),   height = 350 )

title: Axis & Grid
text: Add horizontal /vertical grid lines grid prop:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets")   ),   grid   = list(horizontal = TRUE, vertical = TRUE),   height = 300 )

title: Axis & Grid
text: Use ChartsReferenceLine mark important thresholds. saga average character count shown:
code: avg_chars <- starwars_films |>   mutate(n = lengths(characters)) |>   summarise(avg = round(mean(n))) |>   pull(avg)  ChartDataProvider(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |>     arrange(episode_id),   height = 350,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters")),   ChartsTooltip(),   ChartsSurface(     ChartsGrid(horizontal = TRUE),     BarPlot(),     ChartsReferenceLine(       y     = avg_chars,       label = paste("Saga avg:", avg_chars),       lineStyle = list(stroke = "red", strokeDasharray = "5 5")     ),     ChartsXAxis(),     ChartsYAxis()   ) )

title: Axis & Grid
text: Control spacing SVG edge drawing area margin prop:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters")),   margin = list(top = 20, bottom = 60, left = 80, right = 20),   height = 300 )

title: Axis & Grid
text: details axis customization, visit MUI X Axis documentation.

title: Bar Charts
text: Bar charts express quantities bar’s length, using common baseline. ideal comparing discrete categories excel visualizing differences magnitude across categories, highlighting trends, comparing proportions glance.

title: Bar Charts
code: library(muiCharts) library(dplyr) library(tidyr)

title: Bar Charts
text: vertical bar chart showing six tallest Star Wars characters starwars_people. Sorting slicing puts tallest first:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_people |> head(4),   xAxis = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "name", label = "Character")),   series = list(list(dataKey = "height", label = "Height (cm)")),   height = 300 )

title: Bar Charts
text: Use layout = "horizontal" category names long. starwars_starships sorted trimmed eight largest vessels — names read naturally y-axis:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_people |> head(4) |> arrange(height),   layout = "horizontal",   yAxis = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "name", label = "Character", width = 140)),   series = list(list(dataKey = "height", label = "Height (m)")),   height = 380 )

title: Bar Charts
text: Place multiple series side side compare directly. chart contrasts height mass first six characters values known:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_people |> head(4),   xAxis = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "name", label = "Character")),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "height", label = "Height (cm)"),     list(dataKey = "mass",   label = "Mass (kg)")   ),   grid = list(horizontal = TRUE),   height = 300 )

title: Bar Charts
text: Stack series stack show totals part--whole relationships. starwars_films dataset contains list columns; lengths() counts many items film :
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_films |> mutate(     Characters = lengths(characters),     Planets    = lengths(planets),     Species    = lengths(species)   ),   layout = "horizontal",   yAxis = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "title", width = 220)),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Number of Characters", stack = "total"),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Number of Planets",    stack = "total"),     list(dataKey = "Species",    label = "Number of Species",    stack = "total")   ),   height = 320 )

title: Bar Charts
text: Set barLabel = "value" print data value directly bar. top species average height starwars_species make compact, readable chart:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_species |>     filter(!is.na(average_height)) |>     head(4),   xAxis = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "name", label = "Species")),   series = list(list(     dataKey  = "average_height",     label    = "Avg. Height (cm)",     barLabel = "value"   )),   height = 320 )

title: Bar Charts
text: Integer columns work discrete band categories. release_date converted integer year — unique year gets band, valueFormatter prevents MUI formatting 1977 1,977:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_films |> mutate(     year         = as.integer(format(release_date, "%Y")),     n_characters = lengths(characters)   ),   xAxis = list(list(     scaleType      = "band",     dataKey        = "year",     label          = "Film Release Year",     valueFormatter = JS("(v) => String(v)")   )),   series = list(list(dataKey = "n_characters", label = "Number of Characters")),   grid = list(horizontal = TRUE),   height = 300 )

title: Bar Charts
text: Set color series borderRadius chart polished look. film character counts starwars_species list columns styled coordinated purple palette:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_species |>     mutate(n_films = lengths(films), n_people = lengths(people)) |>     arrange(desc(n_films)) |>     head(4),   xAxis = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "name", label = "Species")),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "n_films",  label = "Number of Films",      color = "#4338CA"),     list(dataKey = "n_people", label = "Number of Characters", color = "#A5B4FC")   ),   borderRadius = 8,   grid = list(horizontal = TRUE),   height = 320 )

title: Bar Charts
text: Assign different stack values create independent stacks side side. characters grouped gender: "cast" stack shows many characters exist per gender, "piloted" stack breaks vehicles operated — starships ground vehicles stacked separately:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_people |>     filter(gender %in% c("male", "female")) |>     group_by(gender) |>     summarise(       Characters = n(),       Starships  = sum(lengths(starships)),       Vehicles   = sum(lengths(vehicles))     ),   xAxis = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "gender", label = "Gender")),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters",        stack = "cast"),     list(dataKey = "Starships",  label = "Starships piloted", stack = "piloted"),     list(dataKey = "Vehicles",   label = "Vehicles piloted",  stack = "piloted")   ),   grid = list(horizontal = TRUE),   height = 320 )

title: Bar Charts
text: BarElement BarLabel sub-components BarPlot uses internally render bar label. Call directly explore props: Use slotProps BarPlot pass props components without custom slot override:
code: BarElement(skipAnimation = TRUE) BarLabel(placement = "outside") ChartDataProvider(   dataset = starwars_species |>     filter(!is.na(average_height)) |>     head(4) |>     arrange(desc(average_height)),   height = 340,   xAxis = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "name", label = "Species")),   series = list(list(type = "bar", dataKey = "average_height", label = "Avg. Height (cm)")),   ChartsTooltip(),   ChartsLegend(),   ChartsSurface(     BarPlot(       barLabel = "value",       slotProps = list(barLabel = list(placement = "outside"))     ),     ChartsXAxis(),     ChartsYAxis()   ) )

title: Bar Charts
text: muiCharts integrates seamlessly muiMaterial create modern, fully-styled dashboards. muiMaterial’s Card, Typography, Stack components make easy add titles, subtitles, footers around charts. Combine ThemeProvider customize colors across entire dashboard: Stack component organizes title, chart, footer. primaryColor ThemeProvider reused chart. pattern works muiCharts component fits perfectly within Material Design ecosystem.
code: library(muiMaterial)  muiMaterialPage(   CssBaseline(),   {     primaryColor <- "#1976d2"      ThemeProvider(       theme = list(palette = list(primary = list(main = primaryColor))),       Box(sx = list(p = 3, bgcolor = "background.paper"),         Card(sx = list(boxShadow = 3),           CardContent(             Stack(spacing = 1.5,               Typography("Character Count", variant = "h6", sx = list(fontWeight = 600)),               Typography("Number of characters by episode", variant = "body2", sx = list(color = "text.secondary")),               BarChart(                 dataset = starwars_films |> mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |> arrange(episode_id),                 xAxis = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id")),                 series = list(list(dataKey = "Characters", color = primaryColor)),                 height = 280               ),               Divider(),               Typography("Made with muiCharts and muiMaterial", variant = "caption", sx = list(color = "text.secondary"))             )           )         )       )     )   } )

title: Bar Charts
text: MUI Bar Charts Documentation See also: Tooltip value formatters, Styling colors, Composition advanced layout, Axis & Grid grid axis options.

title: Composition
text: MUI X Charts can built using self-contained components (e.g. BarChart) composed individual building blocks. composition API gives full control layout, sub-components, mixed chart types.

title: Composition
code: library(muiCharts) library(muiMaterial) library(dplyr)

title: Composition
text: Self-contained components like BarChart simple use, composition API gives : Full control sub-components rendered Ability mix chart types (lines + bars one chart) Custom layout HTML components (legend, tooltip) Adding reference lines, custom text, overlays

title: Composition
text: Three key components work together: ChartDataProvider — wraps everything, provides data context ChartsSurface — renders SVG element (plots, axes, grid) ChartsLegend / ChartsTooltip — HTML components placed outside SVG
code: ChartDataProvider(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets),       Species    = lengths(species),       Starships  = lengths(starships)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   height = 300,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),     list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets"),     list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Species",    label = "Species"),     list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Starships",  label = "Starships")   ),   ChartsLegend(),   ChartsTooltip(),   ChartsSurface(     ChartsGrid(horizontal = TRUE),     BarPlot(),     ChartsXAxis(),     ChartsYAxis()   ) )

title: Composition
text: Use muiMaterial components custom titles layout:
code: library(muiMaterial)  Box(   sx = list(width = "100%", display = "flex", flexDirection = "column", alignItems = "center"),   Typography(variant = "h6", "Star Wars Film Elements by Episode"),   ChartDataProvider(     dataset = starwars_films |>       mutate(         Characters = lengths(characters),         Planets    = lengths(planets)       ) |>       arrange(episode_id),     height = 300,     xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),     series = list(       list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),       list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets")     ),     ChartsLegend(),     ChartsTooltip(),     ChartsSurface(       ChartsGrid(horizontal = TRUE),       BarPlot(),       ChartsXAxis(),       ChartsYAxis()     )   ) )

title: Composition
text: Combine bar line plots single chart — bars show character counts, line tracks planet counts:
code: ChartDataProvider(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   height = 350,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(type = "bar",  dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters (bars)"),     list(type = "line", dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets (line)")   ),   ChartsLegend(),   ChartsTooltip(),   ChartsSurface(     ChartsGrid(horizontal = TRUE),     BarPlot(),     LinePlot(),     MarkPlot(),     ChartsXAxis(),     ChartsYAxis()   ) )

title: Composition
text: Mark important thresholds ChartsReferenceLine:
code: avg_chars <- starwars_films |>   mutate(n = lengths(characters)) |>   summarise(avg = round(mean(n))) |>   pull(avg)  ChartDataProvider(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |>     arrange(episode_id),   height = 350,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters")),   ChartsTooltip(),   ChartsSurface(     ChartsGrid(horizontal = TRUE),     BarPlot(),     ChartsReferenceLine(       y     = avg_chars,       label = paste("Saga avg:", avg_chars),       lineStyle = list(stroke = "red", strokeDasharray = "5 5")     ),     ChartsXAxis(),     ChartsYAxis()   ) )

title: Composition
text: Compose scatter chart title custom grid — height vs mass characters:
code: Box(   sx = list(width = "100%", display = "flex", flexDirection = "column", alignItems = "center"),   Typography(variant = "h6", "Star Wars Characters: Height vs Mass"),   ChartDataProvider(     dataset = starwars_people |>       filter(!is.na(height), !is.na(mass), mass < 200) |>       mutate(id = row_number()),     height = 350,     series = list(list(       type        = "scatter",       datasetKeys = list(x = "height", y = "mass"),       label       = "Characters"     )),     xAxis = list(list(label = "Height (cm)")),     yAxis = list(list(label = "Mass (kg)")),     ChartsLegend(),     ChartsTooltip(trigger = "item"),     ChartsSurface(       ChartsGrid(horizontal = TRUE, vertical = TRUE),       ScatterPlot(),       ChartsXAxis(),       ChartsYAxis()     )   ) )

title: Composition
text: Compose line chart area fill interactive highlighting:
code: ChartDataProvider(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   height = 350,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(type = "line", dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters", area = TRUE),     list(type = "line", dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets",    area = TRUE)   ),   ChartsLegend(),   ChartsTooltip(trigger = "axis"),   ChartsSurface(     ChartsGrid(horizontal = TRUE),     AreaPlot(),     LinePlot(),     MarkPlot(),     LineHighlightPlot(),     ChartsAxisHighlight(x = "line"),     ChartsXAxis(),     ChartsYAxis()   ) )

title: Composition
text: Compose gauge individual sub-components full control:
code: GaugeContainer(   width        = 200,   height       = 200,   value        = 75,   startAngle   = -110,   endAngle     = 110,   innerRadius  = "60%",   outerRadius  = "100%",   cornerRadius = "50%",   GaugeReferenceArc(),   GaugeValueArc(),   GaugeValueText(text = JS("({ value, valueMax }) => `${value} / ${valueMax}`")) )

title: Composition
text: ChartsDataProvider provides data context without rendering SVG — pair ChartsWrapper ChartsSurface: ChartsContainer combines ChartsDataProvider ChartsSurface — useful need SVG content:
code: ChartsDataProvider(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   height = 300,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),     list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets")   ),   ChartsWrapper(     ChartsLegend(),     ChartsTooltip(),     ChartsSurface(       ChartsGrid(horizontal = TRUE),       BarPlot(),       ChartsXAxis(),       ChartsYAxis()     )   ) ) ChartsContainer(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   height = 300,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),     list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets")   ),   ChartsGrid(horizontal = TRUE),   BarPlot(),   ChartsAxisHighlight(x = "band"),   ChartsXAxis(),   ChartsYAxis() )

title: Composition
text: ChartsLabel renders styled HTML <span> element. Place sibling ChartsSurface inside ChartDataProvider:
code: ChartDataProvider(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |>     arrange(episode_id),   height = 320,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters")),   ChartsLabel("Characters per Film", style = list(fontSize = "0.85rem", fontWeight = "bold")),   ChartsSurface(     BarPlot(),     ChartsXAxis(),     ChartsYAxis()   ) )

title: Composition
text: ChartsText renders SVG text element inside ChartsSurface — useful annotations directly chart:
code: ChartDataProvider(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |>     arrange(episode_id),   height = 320,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters")),   ChartsSurface(     BarPlot(),     ChartsText(       text  = "Characters per episode",       x     = 5,       y     = 15,       style = list(fontSize = "0.75rem", fill = "#555")     ),     ChartsXAxis(),     ChartsYAxis()   ) )

title: Composition
text: details composition, visit MUI X Composition documentation.

title: Gauge Charts
text: Gauge charts let users evaluate single metric relative defined range. article demonstrates gauge charts muiCharts based MUI X Gauge documentation.

title: Gauge Charts
code: library(muiCharts) library(dplyr)

title: Gauge Charts
text: Gauge displays numeric value 0 100 default. Control arc range startAngle endAngle:
code: # Percentage of characters whose gender is "male" male_pct <- starwars_people |>   summarise(pct = round(mean(gender == "male", na.rm = TRUE) * 100)) |>   pull(pct)  # Full circle gauge Gauge(width = 100, height = 100, value = male_pct) # Semi-circle gauge (speedometer style) Gauge(width = 100, height = 100, value = male_pct, startAngle = -90, endAngle = 90)

title: Gauge Charts
text: Use valueMin valueMax shift gauge 0–100 default — average character height mapped 100–250 cm range:
code: avg_height <- starwars_people |>   filter(!is.na(height)) |>   summarise(avg = round(mean(height))) |>   pull(avg)  # Default range Gauge(width = 100, height = 100, value = 50) # Custom range (100–250 cm) Gauge(width = 100, height = 100, value = avg_height, valueMin = 100, valueMax = 250)

title: Gauge Charts
text: Modify arc shape innerRadius, outerRadius, cornerRadius:
code: # Data completeness: % of characters with known mass mass_known <- starwars_people |>   summarise(pct = round(mean(!is.na(mass)) * 100)) |>   pull(pct)  # Donut gauge Gauge(width = 150, height = 150, value = mass_known, innerRadius = "70%", outerRadius = "100%") # With rounded corners Gauge(width = 150, height = 150, value = mass_known, innerRadius = "70%", outerRadius = "100%", cornerRadius = "50%")

title: Gauge Charts
text: Customize center text text prop — static string JavaScript formatter:
code: # % of characters with known height height_known <- starwars_people |>   summarise(pct = round(mean(!is.na(height)) * 100)) |>   pull(pct)  # Static text Gauge(width = 150, height = 150, value = height_known, text = paste0(height_known, "%")) # Dynamic formatter Gauge(width = 150, height = 150, value = height_known, text = JS("({ value, valueMax }) => `${value} / ${valueMax}`"))

title: Gauge Charts
text: Customize colors using sx prop MUI CSS class selectors:
code: Gauge(   width        = 150,   height       = 150,   value        = male_pct,   cornerRadius = "50%",   sx = list(     "& .MuiGauge-valueText"   = list(fontSize = 40),     "& .MuiGauge-valueArc"    = list(fill = "#52b202"),     "& .MuiGauge-referenceArc"= list(fill = "#e0e0e0")   ) )

title: Gauge Charts
text: Different arc ranges suit different use cases:
code: # % of species with a known homeworld homeworld_known <- starwars_species |>   summarise(pct = round(mean(!is.na(homeworld)) * 100)) |>   pull(pct)  # Semi-circle Gauge(width = 200, height = 120, value = homeworld_known, startAngle = -90, endAngle = 90) # Quarter-circle Gauge(width = 150, height = 150, value = homeworld_known, startAngle = 0, endAngle = 90)

title: Gauge Charts
text: dashboard showing three data-quality metrics starwars_people:
code: library(muiMaterial)  height_known <- round(mean(!is.na(starwars_people$height)) * 100) mass_known   <- round(mean(!is.na(starwars_people$mass))   * 100) birth_known  <- round(mean(!is.na(starwars_people$birth_year)) * 100)  Box(   sx = list(display = "flex", gap = 2, justifyContent = "center", alignItems = "center"),   Box(     sx = list(display = "flex", flexDirection = "column", alignItems = "center"),     Typography(variant = "subtitle2", "Height known"),     Gauge(width = 100, height = 100, value = height_known)   ),   Box(     sx = list(display = "flex", flexDirection = "column", alignItems = "center"),     Typography(variant = "subtitle2", "Mass known"),     Gauge(width = 100, height = 100, value = mass_known)   ),   Box(     sx = list(display = "flex", flexDirection = "column", alignItems = "center"),     Typography(variant = "subtitle2", "Birth year known"),     Gauge(width = 100, height = 100, value = birth_known)   ) )

title: Gauge Charts
text: Gauge follows ARIA Meter pattern. Add aria-label aria-labelledby screen readers:
code: species_count <- nrow(starwars_species) diversity_pct <- min(round(species_count / 50 * 100), 100)  Box(   Typography(id = "div_label", variant = "h6", "Species Diversity Index"),   Gauge(     width            = 150,     height           = 150,     value            = diversity_pct,     `aria-labelledby`= "div_label",     `aria-valuetext` = paste0(species_count, " species out of 50")   ) )

title: Gauge Charts
text: Use GaugeContainer individual sub-components full control layout: Customize center text JavaScript formatter:
code: GaugeContainer(   width       = 200,   height      = 200,   value       = male_pct,   startAngle  = -110,   endAngle    = 110,   GaugeReferenceArc(),   GaugeValueArc(),   GaugeValueText() ) GaugeContainer(   width        = 200,   height       = 200,   value        = male_pct,   startAngle   = -110,   endAngle     = 110,   innerRadius  = "60%",   outerRadius  = "100%",   cornerRadius = "50%",   GaugeReferenceArc(),   GaugeValueArc(),   GaugeValueText(text = JS("({ value, valueMax }) => `${value} / ${valueMax}`")) )

title: Gauge Charts
text: MUI Gauge Chart Documentation See also: Styling sx overrides arc styling.

title: Highlighting
text: Highlighting provides visual feedback users interact chart elements. article demonstrates highlighting options based MUI Highlighting documentation.

title: Highlighting
text: Element counts per film — used throughout vignette:
code: library(muiCharts) library(dplyr) film_counts <- starwars_films |>   mutate(     Characters = lengths(characters),     Planets    = lengths(planets),     Species    = lengths(species)   ) |>   arrange(episode_id)

title: Highlighting
text: highlightScope prop controls items emphasized hover. two properties: highlight — items highlight: "item", "series", "none" fade — items fade: "global", "series", "none"

title: Highlighting
text: Highlight single bar hover, fading others:
code: BarChart(   dataset = film_counts,   layout  = "horizontal",   yAxis   = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "title", width = 220)),   series  = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters", highlightScope = list(highlight = "item", fade = "global")),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets",    highlightScope = list(highlight = "item", fade = "global"))   ),   height = 320 )

title: Highlighting
text: Highlight entire series hovering bar :
code: BarChart(   dataset = film_counts,   layout  = "horizontal",   yAxis   = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "title", width = 220)),   series  = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters", highlightScope = list(highlight = "series", fade = "global")),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets",    highlightScope = list(highlight = "series", fade = "global")),     list(dataKey = "Species",    label = "Species",    highlightScope = list(highlight = "series", fade = "global"))   ),   height = 320 )

title: Highlighting
text: Customize appearance faded slices pie charts using faded property:
code: PieChart(   series = list(list(     data = starwars_people |>       count(gender) |>       filter(!is.na(gender)) |>       select(label = gender, value = n),     highlightScope = list(highlight = "item", fade = "global"),     faded = list(innerRadius = 30, additionalRadius = -30, color = "gray")   )),   height = 300 )

title: Highlighting
text: Highlighting works across chart types. line series highlights hover:
code: LineChart(   dataset = film_counts,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters", highlightScope = list(highlight = "series", fade = "global")),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets",    highlightScope = list(highlight = "series", fade = "global")),     list(dataKey = "Species",    label = "Species",    highlightScope = list(highlight = "series", fade = "global"))   ),   height = 300 )

title: Highlighting
text: details highlighting, visit MUI X Highlighting documentation.

title: Introduction
text: can install development version muiCharts like :
code: remotes::install_github("lgnbhl/muiCharts")

title: Introduction
code: library(muiCharts) library(dplyr) BarChart(   dataset = head(starwars_people, 4),   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "name")),   series = list(list(dataKey = "height", label = "Height (cm)")),   height = 300 )

title: Introduction
text: Use JS() function pass JavaScript code, example customize tooltip values valueFormatter:
code: BarChart(   dataset = head(starwars_people, 4),   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "name")),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "height", label = "Height (cm)", valueFormatter = JS("(v) => v + ' cm'")),     list(dataKey = "mass",   label = "Mass (kg)",   valueFormatter = JS("(v) => v + ' kg'"))   ),   height = 300 )

title: Introduction
text: muiCharts integrates seamlessly muiMaterial create modern, fully-styled dashboards.
code: library(muiMaterial)  muiMaterialPage(   CssBaseline(),   {     primaryColor <- "#1976d2"      ThemeProvider(       theme = list(palette = list(primary = list(main = primaryColor))),       Box(sx = list(p = 3, bgcolor = "background.paper"),         Card(sx = list(boxShadow = 3),           CardContent(             Stack(spacing = 1.5,               Typography("Character Count", variant = "h6", sx = list(fontWeight = 600)),               Typography("Number of characters by Star Wars episode", variant = "body2", sx = list(color = "text.secondary")),               BarChart(                 dataset = starwars_films |> mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |> arrange(episode_id),                 xAxis = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id")),                 series = list(list(dataKey = "Characters", color = primaryColor)),                 height = 280               ),               Divider(),               Typography("Made with muiCharts and muiMaterial", variant = "caption", sx = list(color = "text.secondary"))             )           )         )       )     )   } )

title: Introduction
text: muiCharts provides following chart types, dedicated article: Bar Charts — vertical horizontal bar charts grouping stacking Line Charts — line charts marks, curves, null handling Area Charts — filled area charts stacking baselines Pie Charts — pie donut charts arc labels Scatter Charts — scatter plots Voronoi interaction Radar Charts — radar charts multi-metric comparisons Sparkline Charts — compact inline charts Gauge Charts — gauge displays single metrics

title: Introduction
text: Cross-cutting features apply chart types: Axis & Grid — scale types, labels, value formatters, grid lines, margins Tooltip — tooltip triggers, formatting, composition Legend — legend position, direction, color legends Highlighting — highlight fade interactions Styling — colors, color maps, CSS overrides sx Composition — build charts individual sub-components

title: Introduction
text: Shiny — reactive charts, click events, dashboards Quarto — HTML documents, dashboards, tabsets

title: Introduction
text: issue, question want contribute pull request, don’t hesitate write https://github.com/lgnbhl/muiCharts updates follow Felix Luginbuhl LinkedIn.

title: Legend
text: legend identifies series displayed chart. article demonstrates legend customization based MUI Legend documentation.

title: Legend
code: library(muiCharts) library(dplyr)

title: Legend
text: default, legend displayed chart showing series:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets),       Species    = lengths(species),       Starships  = lengths(starships)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets"),     list(dataKey = "Species",    label = "Species"),     list(dataKey = "Starships",  label = "Starships")   ),   height = 300 )

title: Legend
text: Use hideLegend = TRUE remove legend entirely:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis      = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series     = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets")   ),   hideLegend = TRUE,   height     = 300 )

title: Legend
text: Position legend using slotProps. argument horizontal accepts "start", "middle", "end", vertical accepts "top", "middle", "bottom":
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets")   ),   slotProps = list(legend = list(     position = list(vertical = "bottom", horizontal = "middle")   )),   height = 300 )

title: Legend
text: Change legend layout horizontal (default) vertical:
code: BarChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets),       Species    = lengths(species),       Starships  = lengths(starships)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets"),     list(dataKey = "Species",    label = "Species"),     list(dataKey = "Starships",  label = "Starships")   ),   slotProps = list(legend = list(direction = "vertical")),   height = 350 )

title: Legend
text: Pie charts automatically show legend entries slice:
code: PieChart(   series = list(list(     data = starwars_people |>       count(gender) |>       filter(!is.na(gender)) |>       select(label = gender, value = n)   )),   height = 300 )

title: Legend
text: charts using colorMap, use ContinuousColorLegend PiecewiseColorLegend explain color scale.

title: Legend
text: Characters plotted height mass, colored birth year:
code: ScatterChart(   height  = 380,   dataset = starwars_people |>     filter(!is.na(height), !is.na(mass), !is.na(birth_year), mass < 200) |>     mutate(id = row_number()),   series  = list(list(     datasetKeys = list(x = "height", y = "mass", z = "birth_year"),     label = "Characters"   )),   xAxis  = list(list(label = "Height (cm)")),   yAxis  = list(list(label = "Mass (kg)")),   zAxis  = list(list(     colorMap = list(type = "continuous", min = 0, max = 100, color = c("#d01c8b", "#4dac26"))   )) )

title: Legend
text: using composition, place ChartsLegend inside ChartDataProvider outside ChartsSurface (legend HTML, SVG):
code: library(muiMaterial)  ChartDataProvider(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   height = 300,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),     list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets")   ),   ChartsLegend(),   ChartsSurface(     BarPlot(),     ChartsXAxis(),     ChartsYAxis()   ) )

title: Legend
text: ChartsLabelMark renders small colored shape appears next series name. Use custom legend layouts:
code: ChartDataProvider(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   height = 300,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),     list(type = "bar", dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets")   ),   Box(     sx = list(display = "flex", gap = "16px", padding = "8px"),     Box(       sx = list(display = "flex", alignItems = "center", gap = "4px"),       ChartsLabelMark(type = "square", color = "#1976d2"),       Typography(variant = "body2", "Characters")     ),     Box(       sx = list(display = "flex", alignItems = "center", gap = "4px"),       ChartsLabelMark(type = "square", color = "#d32f2f"),       Typography(variant = "body2", "Planets")     )   ),   ChartsSurface(     BarPlot(),     ChartsXAxis(),     ChartsYAxis()   ) )

title: Legend
text: details legend customization, visit MUI X Legend documentation.

title: Line Charts
text: Line charts ideal showing values change continuous dimensions time episode sequence. emphasize trends, patterns, fluctuations, making useful exploring relationships tracking progress time.

title: Line Charts
code: library(muiCharts) library(dplyr)

title: Line Charts
text: Element counts per Star Wars film episode order — characters, planets, species:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets),       Species    = lengths(species)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(     list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")   ),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Number of Characters"),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Number of Planets"),     list(dataKey = "Species",    label = "Number of Species")   ),   height = 300 )

title: Line Charts
text: Use release year continuous x-axis reflect real-world chronology:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       year       = as.integer(format(release_date, "%Y")),       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(year),   xAxis = list(list(     scaleType      = "linear",     dataKey        = "year",     label          = "Film Release Year",     valueFormatter = JS("(v) => String(v)")   )),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),     list(dataKey = "Planets", label = "Planets")   ),   grid   = list(horizontal = TRUE),   height = 300 )

title: Line Charts
text: Show individual data points line showMark = TRUE. adding scaleType = "band", can add character name marks. character heights sorted name:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_people |> head(5),   xAxis  = list(     list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "name")   ),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "height", label = "Height (cm)", showMark = TRUE)   ),   height = 320 )

title: Line Charts
text: Hide marks cleaner look multiple overlapping series:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets),       Species    = lengths(species)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(     list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")   ),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Number of Characters", showMark = FALSE),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Number of Planets",    showMark = FALSE),     list(dataKey = "Species",    label = "Number of Species",    showMark = FALSE)   ),   grid   = list(horizontal = TRUE, vertical = TRUE),   height = 300 )

title: Line Charts
text: Control line shape curve property. "natural" produces smooth curves; "linear" draws straight segments:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters", curve = "natural", showMark = TRUE),     list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets",    curve = "linear",  showMark = TRUE)   ),   height = 300 )

title: Line Charts
text: Use curve = "step" categorical data values jump discretely — starship passenger capacity size order:
code: LineChart(   dataset = starwars_people |> head(4),   xAxis  = list(     list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "name", width = "auto")   ),   series = list(     list(dataKey = "height", label = "Height", curve = "step")   ),   height = 320 )

title: Line Charts
text: LineElement, MarkElement, AnimatedLine sub-components rendered LinePlot MarkPlot. Use slotProps configure composition chart:
code: ChartDataProvider(   dataset = starwars_films |>     mutate(       Characters = lengths(characters),       Planets    = lengths(planets)     ) |>     arrange(episode_id),   height = 300,   xAxis  = list(list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),   series = list(     list(type = "line", dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),     list(type = "line", dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets")   ),   ChartsTooltip(),   ChartsSurface(     LinePlot(slotProps = list(line = list(skipAnimation = TRUE))),     MarkPlot(slotProps = list(mark = list(shape = "circle"))),     ChartsXAxis(),     ChartsYAxis()   ) )

title: Line Charts
text: muiCharts integrates seamlessly muiMaterial create modern, fully-styled dashboards. muiMaterial’s Card, Typography, Stack components make easy add titles, subtitles, footers around charts. Combine ThemeProvider customize colors across entire dashboard: Stack component organizes title, chart, footer. Colors ThemeProvider reused chart. pattern works muiCharts component fits perfectly within Material Design ecosystem.
code: library(muiMaterial)  muiMaterialPage(   CssBaseline(),   {     primaryColor <- "#1976d2"     secondaryColor <- "#dc004e"      ThemeProvider(       theme = list(palette = list(primary = list(main = primaryColor), secondary = list(main = secondaryColor))),       Box(sx = list(p = 3, bgcolor = "background.paper"),         Card(sx = list(boxShadow = 3),           CardContent(             Stack(spacing = 1.5,               Typography("Film Elements Across Episodes", variant = "h6", sx = list(fontWeight = 600)),               Typography("Characters and planets by episode", variant = "body2", sx = list(color = "text.secondary")),               LineChart(                 dataset = starwars_films |> mutate(Characters = lengths(characters), Planets = lengths(planets)) |> arrange(episode_id),                 xAxis = list(list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id")),                 series = list(                   list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters", color = primaryColor),                   list(dataKey = "Planets", label = "Planets", color = secondaryColor)                 ),                 height = 280               ),               Divider(),               Typography("Made with muiCharts and muiMaterial", variant = "caption", sx = list(color = "text.secondary"))             )           )         )       )     )   } )

title: Line Charts
text: MUI Line Charts Documentation See also: Tooltip value formatters, Styling colors, Composition advanced layout, Axis & Grid grid axis options.

title: Pie Charts
text: Pie charts express portions whole using arcs angles within circle. ideal showing proportions making relative shares easy compare glance. slice represents category’s contribution total. Note: data argument PieChart() expects data frame least two columns named label (slice names) value (numeric sizes). additional columns (e.g. color) passed per-slice properties.

title: Pie Charts
code: library(muiCharts) library(dplyr)

title: Pie Charts
text: Top 4 species number members:
code: PieChart(   series = list(     list(       data = starwars_people |>          count(gender, sort = TRUE) |>         filter(!is.na(gender)) |>         rename(label = gender, value = n)       )   ),   height = 300 )

title: Pie Charts
text: Add color column assign colors individual slices:
code: PieChart(   series = list(     list(       data = starwars_species |> head(4) |>         transmute(           label = name,             value = lengths(people),           color = c("#4338CA", "#7C3AED", "#A78BFA", "#DDD6FE"))     )   ),   height = 300 )

title: Pie Charts
text: Create donut chart setting innerRadius:
code: PieChart(   series = list(     list(       data = starwars_species |> head(4) |>       transmute(label = name, value = lengths(people)),     innerRadius = 50,     outerRadius = 100     )   ),   height = 300 )

title: Pie Charts
text: Display labels directly slices arcLabel:
code: PieChart(   series = list(     list(       data = starwars_species |> head(4) |>         transmute(label = name, value = lengths(people)),       arcLabel = "label",       innerRadius = 50,       outerRadius = 100     )   ),   height = 300 )

title: Pie Charts
text: Show count arc using arcLabel = "value":
code: PieChart(   series = list(     list(       data = starwars_species |> head(4) |>         transmute(label = name, value = lengths(people)),       arcLabel = "value",       innerRadius = 40,       outerRadius = 100     )   ),   height = 300 )

title: Pie Charts
text: Use arcLabel JavaScript formatter show percentage slice:
code: total <- sum(lengths(head(starwars_species, 4)$people))  PieChart(   series = list(     list(       data = starwars_species |> head(4) |>         transmute(label = name, value = lengths(people)),         arcLabel = JS(paste0("(item) => Math.round(item.value / ", total, " * 100) + '%'")),         arcLabelMinAngle = 20,         innerRadius = 50,         outerRadius = 100     )   ),   height = 300 )

title: Pie Charts
text: Add spacing slices paddingAngle rounded corners cornerRadius:
code: PieChart(   series = list(     list(       data = starwars_species |> head(4) |>         transmute(label = name, value = lengths(people)),       paddingAngle = 5,       cornerRadius = 5,       innerRadius  = 40,       outerRadius  = 100     )   ),   height = 300 )

title: Pie Charts
text: Restrict arc range startAngle endAngle:
code: PieChart(   series = list(     list(       data = starwars_species |> head(4) |>         transmute(label = name, value = lengths(people)),       startAngle = -90,       endAngle =  90,       innerRadius = 50,       outerRadius = 100     )   ),   height = 250 )

title: Pie Charts
text: semi-donut showing share characters female:
code: female_pct <- starwars_people |>   summarise(pct = round(mean(gender == "female", na.rm = TRUE) * 100)) |>   pull(pct)  PieChart(   series = list(list(     data = data.frame(       label = c("Female", "Other"),       value = c(female_pct, 100 - female_pct)     ),     innerRadius = 70,     outerRadius = 90,     startAngle  = -90,     endAngle    =  90   )),   height = 250 )

title: Pie Charts
text: Build pie charts individual sub-components using ChartDataProvider PiePlot:

title: Quarto
text: muiCharts uses MUI (Material UI) rendering, can conflict Bootstrap CSS bundled Quarto. may cause visual issues broken layouts, unexpected margins, wrong font sizing.

title: Quarto
text: simplest fix disable Bootstrap entirely setting minimal: true YAML header: removes Bootstrap Quarto styling features (navbar, footer, search, etc.), giving MUI full control chart rendering. recommended approach standalone chart documents.
code: --- title: "My Charts" format:   html:     minimal: true ---

title: Quarto
text: want keep Quarto features disable Bootstrap, use theme: none: Note theme: none still strips Quarto structural elements depend Bootstrap (navigation, footer, logo). similar minimal: true sets theme: none automatically.
code: --- title: "My Charts" format:   html:     theme: none ---

title: Quarto
text: need keep Bootstrap (e.g., Quarto websites dashboards), can add targeted CSS fix specific conflicts: custom.css, scope overrides MUI chart containers: exact overrides needed depend Bootstrap styles conflict charts. Inspect rendered output identify specific issues.
code: --- title: "My Charts" format:   html:     css: custom.css --- .mui-charts-widget * {   box-sizing: content-box; } .mui-charts-widget label {   margin-bottom: 0; }

title: Radar Charts
text: Radar charts compare multiple quantitative variables one groups. ideal profiling items across common set dimensions.

title: Radar Charts
text: Compute element counts per film — Characters, Planets, Species Starships make natural radar dimensions:
code: library(muiCharts) library(dplyr) film_metrics <- starwars_films |>   mutate(     n_characters = lengths(characters),     n_planets    = lengths(planets),     n_species    = lengths(species),     n_starships  = lengths(starships)   )

title: Radar Charts
text: Profile single film across four element dimensions:
code: RadarChart(   height  = 300,   series  = list(     list(       label = film_metrics$title[1],        data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[1], film_metrics$n_planets[1],         film_metrics$n_species[1], film_metrics$n_starships[1]       )     )   ),   radar   = list(metrics = c("Characters", "Planets", "Species", "Starships")) )

title: Radar Charts
text: Overlay multiple films compare composed across dimensions:
code: RadarChart(   height = 350,   series = list(     list(       label = film_metrics$title[1],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[1], film_metrics$n_planets[1],         film_metrics$n_species[1], film_metrics$n_starships[1]       )     ),     list(       label = film_metrics$title[2],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[2], film_metrics$n_planets[2],         film_metrics$n_species[2], film_metrics$n_starships[2]       )     ),     list(       label = film_metrics$title[3],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[3], film_metrics$n_planets[3],         film_metrics$n_species[3], film_metrics$n_starships[3]       )     )   ),   radar  = list(metrics = c("Characters", "Planets", "Species", "Starships")) )

title: Radar Charts
text: Use fillArea = TRUE shade polygon hideMark = TRUE suppress vertex dots:
code: RadarChart(   height = 350,   series = list(     list(       label = film_metrics$title[1],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[1], film_metrics$n_planets[1],         film_metrics$n_species[1], film_metrics$n_starships[1]       ),       fillArea = TRUE     ),     list(       label = film_metrics$title[2],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[2], film_metrics$n_planets[2],         film_metrics$n_species[2], film_metrics$n_starships[2]       ),       hideMark = TRUE     )   ),   radar  = list(metrics = c("Characters", "Planets", "Species", "Starships")) )

title: Radar Charts
text: Define min max per metric ensure axes share scale:
code: RadarChart(   height = 350,   series = list(     list(       label = film_metrics$title[1],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[1], film_metrics$n_planets[1],         film_metrics$n_species[1], film_metrics$n_starships[1]       )     ),     list(       label = film_metrics$title[2],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[2], film_metrics$n_planets[2],         film_metrics$n_species[2], film_metrics$n_starships[2]       )     )   ),   radar  = list(metrics = list(     list(name = "Characters", min = 0, max = 20),     list(name = "Planets",    min = 0, max = 20),     list(name = "Species",    min = 0, max = 20),     list(name = "Starships",  min = 0, max = 20)   )) )

title: Radar Charts
text: Switch circular ("circular") polygon ("sharp") grid:
code: RadarChart(   height    = 350,   series = list(     list(       label = film_metrics$title[1],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[1], film_metrics$n_planets[1],         film_metrics$n_species[1], film_metrics$n_starships[1]       )     ),     list(       label = film_metrics$title[2],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[2], film_metrics$n_planets[2],         film_metrics$n_species[2], film_metrics$n_starships[2]       )     )   ),   shape = "circular",   divisions = 3,   radar = list(metrics = c("Characters", "Planets", "Species", "Starships")) )

title: Radar Charts
text: Control hover highlighting highlight:
code: RadarChart(   height    = 350,   series = list(     list(       label = film_metrics$title[1],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[1], film_metrics$n_planets[1],         film_metrics$n_species[1], film_metrics$n_starships[1]       )     ),     list(       label = film_metrics$title[2],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[2], film_metrics$n_planets[2],         film_metrics$n_species[2], film_metrics$n_starships[2]       )     )   ),   highlight = "series",   radar     = list(metrics = c("Characters", "Planets", "Species", "Starships")) )

title: Radar Charts
text: Customize tooltip behavior trigger option:
code: RadarChart(   height    = 350,   series = list(     list(       label = film_metrics$title[1],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[1], film_metrics$n_planets[1],         film_metrics$n_species[1], film_metrics$n_starships[1]       )     ),     list(       label = film_metrics$title[2],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[2], film_metrics$n_planets[2],         film_metrics$n_species[2], film_metrics$n_starships[2]       )     )   ),   radar     = list(metrics = c("Characters", "Planets", "Species", "Starships")),   slotProps = list(tooltip = list(trigger = "axis")) )

title: Radar Charts
text: RadarDataProvider dedicated data provider radar charts. Use RadarGrid, RadarAxis, RadarSeriesPlot, RadarMetricLabels fully custom layout:
code: RadarDataProvider(   height = 350,   series = list(     list(       label = film_metrics$title[1],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[1], film_metrics$n_planets[1],         film_metrics$n_species[1], film_metrics$n_starships[1]       )     ),     list(       label = film_metrics$title[2],       data = c(         film_metrics$n_characters[2], film_metrics$n_planets[2],         film_metrics$n_species[2], film_metrics$n_starships[2]       )     )   ),   radar  = list(metrics = c("Characters", "Planets", "Species", "Starships")),   ChartsLegend(),   ChartsTooltip(),   ChartsSurface(     RadarGrid(),     RadarSeriesPlot(),     RadarAxis(),     RadarMetricLabels()   ) )

code: library(muiCharts) library(dplyr)

title: Scatter Charts
text: Height vs mass Star Wars characters known values. point character:
code: ScatterChart(   height = 350,   dataset = starwars_people |> head(20),   series = list(list(     datasetKeys = list(x = "height", y = "mass"),     label = "Characters"   )),   xAxis = list(list(label = "Height (cm)")),   yAxis = list(list(label = "Mass (kg)")) )

text: Separate male female characters two series easy comparison: Note: data argument require two columns named x y.

title: Shiny
text: muiCharts uses MUI (Material UI) rendering. Shiny’s default UI functions (fluidPage(), sidebarLayout(), etc.) bslib rely Bootstrap CSS, can conflict MUI styling cause visual issues charts. avoid conflicts, recommend using muiMaterial general layout UI inputs app. Since muiMaterial also built MUI, shares design system muiCharts two work together seamlessly. can still use R Shiny packages (e.g., shiny, bslib, DT, plotly). However, UI elements look different expected, likely due CSS conflicts Bootstrap MUI.

title: Shiny
text: Render muiCharts component inside renderUI():
code: library(muiCharts) library(shiny) library(dplyr)  film_counts <- starwars_films |>   mutate(     Characters = lengths(characters),     Planets    = lengths(planets)   ) |>   arrange(episode_id)  ui <- tagList(uiOutput("chart"))  server <- function(input, output, session) {   output$chart <- renderUI({     BarChart(       dataset = film_counts,       layout  = "horizontal",       yAxis   = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "title", width = 220)),       series  = list(         list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),         list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets")       ),       grid   = list(horizontal = TRUE),       height = 380     )   }) }  shinyApp(ui, server)

title: Shiny
text: Use muiMaterial inputs dynamically update charts — switch chart type select film elements display:
code: library(muiCharts) library(muiMaterial) library(shiny) library(dplyr)  film_counts <- starwars_films |>   mutate(     Characters = lengths(characters),     Planets    = lengths(planets),     Species    = lengths(species),     Starships  = lengths(starships)   ) |>   arrange(episode_id)  ui <- muiMaterialPage(   CssBaseline(     Container(       maxWidth = "md",       sx = list(py = 4),       Typography("Star Wars Films", variant = "h4", gutterBottom = TRUE),       Stack(         spacing = 3,         ToggleButtonGroup.shinyInput(           inputId   = "chart_type",           value     = "bar",           exclusive = TRUE,           ToggleButton.shinyInput(inputId = "btn_bar",  value = "bar",  "Bar"),           ToggleButton.shinyInput(inputId = "btn_line", value = "line", "Line")         ),         Box(           Typography("Elements:", variant = "subtitle1"),           FormGroup(             row = TRUE,             FormControlLabel(               control = Checkbox.shinyInput(inputId = "chk_chars",     value = TRUE),               label   = "Characters"             ),             FormControlLabel(               control = Checkbox.shinyInput(inputId = "chk_planets",   value = TRUE),               label   = "Planets"             ),             FormControlLabel(               control = Checkbox.shinyInput(inputId = "chk_species",   value = FALSE),               label   = "Species"             ),             FormControlLabel(               control = Checkbox.shinyInput(inputId = "chk_starships", value = FALSE),               label   = "Starships"             )           )         ),         uiOutput("chart")       )     )   ) )  server <- function(input, output, session) {   chart_type <- reactiveVal("bar")    observeEvent(input$btn_bar,  chart_type("bar"))   observeEvent(input$btn_line, chart_type("line"))    selected_series <- reactive({     keys <- c(       Characters = input$chk_chars,       Planets    = input$chk_planets,       Species    = input$chk_species,       Starships  = input$chk_starships     )     names(keys[keys == TRUE])   })    output$chart <- renderUI({     req(length(selected_series()) > 0)     series <- lapply(selected_series(), function(s) list(dataKey = s, label = s))      if (chart_type() == "bar") {       BarChart(         dataset = film_counts,         xAxis   = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),         series  = series,         height  = 400       )     } else {       LineChart(         dataset = film_counts,         xAxis   = list(list(scaleType = "point", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),         series  = series,         height  = 400       )     }   }) }  shinyApp(ui, server)

title: Shiny
text: Capture user clicks chart elements using onAxisClick Shiny.setInputValue():
code: library(muiCharts) library(shiny) library(dplyr) library(glue)  film_counts <- starwars_films |>   mutate(     Characters = lengths(characters),     Planets    = lengths(planets)   ) |>   arrange(episode_id)  ui <- tagList(   tags$h1("Click on the chart"),   uiOutput("chart"),   tags$h3("You clicked:"),   tableOutput("clickEvent") )  server <- function(input, output, session) {   setClickEvent <- function(inputId) {     JS(glue::glue("(event, d) => Shiny.setInputValue('{inputId}', d)"))   }    output$clickEvent <- renderTable({     includeNull <- function(x) ifelse(length(x) == 0, NA_character_, paste0(x))     data.frame(       episode    = includeNull(input$userClick$axisValue[1]),       Characters = includeNull(input$userClick$seriesValues$`auto-generated-id-0`[1]),       Planets    = includeNull(input$userClick$seriesValues$`auto-generated-id-1`[1])     )   })    output$chart <- renderUI({     BarChart(       dataset    = film_counts,       onAxisClick = setClickEvent("userClick"),       xAxis      = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),       series     = list(         list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters"),         list(dataKey = "Planets",    label = "Planets")       ),       height = 300     )   }) }  shinyApp(ui, server)

title: Shiny
text: Combine pie chart gauge responsive dashboard layout:
code: library(muiCharts) library(muiMaterial) library(shiny) library(dplyr)  gender_dist <- starwars_people |>   count(gender) |>   filter(!is.na(gender)) |>   select(label = gender, value = n)  male_pct <- round(mean(starwars_people$gender == "male", na.rm = TRUE) * 100)  ui <- muiMaterialPage(   CssBaseline(     Container(       maxWidth = "lg",       sx = list(py = 4),       Typography("Star Wars Metrics", variant = "h4", gutterBottom = TRUE),       Box(         Typography("Chart height:", variant = "subtitle1"),         Slider.shinyInput(           inputId          = "height",           value            = 300,           min              = 200,           max              = 500,           step             = 50,           valueLabelDisplay = "auto",           sx               = list(maxWidth = 300)         )       ),       Grid(         container = TRUE,         spacing   = 3,         sx        = list(mt = 2),         Grid(           size = list(xs = 12, md = 8),           Card(CardContent(             Typography("Characters by Gender", variant = "h6"),             uiOutput("pieChart")           ))         ),         Grid(           size = list(xs = 12, md = 4),           Card(CardContent(             Typography("Male Characters", variant = "h6"),             uiOutput("gauge")           ))         )       )     )   ) )  server <- function(input, output, session) {   output$pieChart <- renderUI({     PieChart(       series = list(list(data = gender_dist, innerRadius = 50, outerRadius = 100)),       height = input$height     )   })    output$gauge <- renderUI({     Gauge(width = 150, height = 150, value = male_pct, startAngle = -90, endAngle = 90)   }) }  shinyApp(ui, server)

title: Shiny

title: Shiny
text: reactOutput() renderReact() alternatives uiOutput() / renderUI() shiny.react package, re-exported convenience:
code: library(muiCharts) library(shiny) library(dplyr)  film_counts <- starwars_films |>   mutate(Characters = lengths(characters)) |>   arrange(episode_id)  ui     <- tagList(reactOutput("chart")) server <- function(input, output, session) {   output$chart <- renderReact({     BarChart(       dataset = film_counts,       xAxis   = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "episode_id", label = "Star Wars Episode")),       series  = list(list(dataKey = "Characters", label = "Characters")),       height  = 300     )   }) }  shinyApp(ui, server)

title: Shiny
text: setInput() programmatically sets Shiny input value server. triggerEvent() fires named event connected JavaScript handlers. re-exported shiny.react:
code: server <- function(input, output, session) {   # Reset a Shiny input value programmatically   observeEvent(input$reset, {     setInput(session, inputId = "selectedIndex", value = 0L)   })    # Trigger a custom event for JavaScript-side chart interactions   observeEvent(input$highlight, {     triggerEvent(session, "chart:highlight", data = list(index = 0L))   }) }

title: Sparkline Charts
text: Sparkline charts provide compact overview data trends without visible axes coordinates. ideal embedding inside tables, cards, dashboards.

title: Sparkline Charts
code: library(muiCharts) library(dplyr)

title: Sparkline Charts
text: SparkLineChart requires data vector numbers. Switch line bars plotType = "bar":
code: # Characters per episode (in chronological order) chars_per_ep <- starwars_films |>   arrange(episode_id) |>   mutate(n = lengths(characters)) |>   pull(n)  # Line sparkline SparkLineChart(data = chars_per_ep, height = 100) # Bar sparkline SparkLineChart(plotType = "bar", data = chars_per_ep, height = 100)

title: Sparkline Charts
text: Fill area curve area = TRUE. Smooth line curve = "natural":
code: SparkLineChart(data = chars_per_ep, height = 100, area = TRUE) SparkLineChart(data = chars_per_ep, height = 100, area = TRUE, curve = "natural")

title: Sparkline Charts
text: Enable showTooltip showHighlight interactive sparklines:
code: SparkLineChart(   data          = chars_per_ep,   height        = 100,   showHighlight = TRUE,   showTooltip   = TRUE )

title: Sparkline Charts
text: Fix y-axis range comparisons sparklines stay fair:
code: # Starships per episode — natural scale ships_per_ep <- starwars_films |>   arrange(episode_id) |>   mutate(n = lengths(starships)) |>   pull(n)  SparkLineChart(data = ships_per_ep, height = 100, showTooltip = TRUE) # Same data, fixed range from 0 to 20 SparkLineChart(data = ships_per_ep, height = 100, yAxis = list(min = 0, max = 20), showTooltip = TRUE)

title: Sparkline Charts
text: Embed sparklines alongside summary statistics inside muiMaterial cards:
code: library(muiMaterial)  Box(   sx = list(display = "flex", flexDirection = "column", gap = 2, padding = 2),   Box(     Typography(variant = "h6", "Characters per Episode"),     Typography(variant = "h4", sum(chars_per_ep)),     SparkLineChart(       data = chars_per_ep,       xAxis = list(         data           = 1:length(chars_per_ep),         valueFormatter = JS(sprintf(           "function(v) { const ep = %s; return 'Ep ' + ep[v-1]; }",           jsonlite::toJSON(starwars_films |> arrange(episode_id) |> pull(episode_id))         ))       ),       height        = 60,       area          = TRUE,       showHighlight = TRUE,       showTooltip   = TRUE     )   ),   Box(     Typography(variant = "h6", "Starships per Episode"),     Typography(variant = "h4", sum(ships_per_ep)),     SparkLineChart(       plotType      = "bar",       data          = ships_per_ep,       height        = 60,       showHighlight = TRUE,       showTooltip   = TRUE     )   ) )
